findings.category: A01: Broken Access Control / Race Condition
findings.title: Unprotected concurrent write to _watchdog_proc global
findings.description: _monitor_tick() reads and writes the _watchdog_proc global without acquiring _cleanup_lock. _do_cleanup() is guarded by _cleanup_lock and calls _signal_watchdog_stop() which uses _session_tmpdir and _stop_token. If cleanup is triggered (SIGTERM/atexit) while the monitor thread is mid-respawn inside _spawn_watchdog(), a race occurs: the monitor thread may spawn a new watchdog process after cleanup has already signalled the old one to stop and potentially deleted the session tmpdir. Result: zombie watchdog process running with a deleted tmpdir, potentially leaving proxy settings unrecovered.
findings.impact: Stale watchdog process spawned post-cleanup; if the new watchdog crashes, no cleanup recovery occurs. Also potential double-spawn if two signals arrive in quick succession.
findings.recommendation:
Acquire _cleanup_lock inside _monitor_tick before the watchdog poll/respawn block, or use a separate dedicated lock for _watchdog_proc. At minimum, guard the respawn path:

with _cleanup_lock:
    if _watchdog_proc and _watchdog_proc.poll() is not None:
        ...respawn...

findings.title: VPN check exceptions silenced at DEBUG level
findings.description: When check_vpn_status() raises an exception, the error is logged at logger.debug() level (line 471). In production deployments where log level is INFO or WARNING, repeated VPN check failures are completely invisible. If the VPN detection mechanism breaks (e.g., WMI error, permission issue), the user will never be notified and VPN disconnection goes undetected — exactly the threat this feature is meant to address.
findings.impact: Silent failure of VPN monitoring: if check_vpn_status() consistently throws, the monitor loop becomes a no-op with no user notification.
findings.recommendation: Log at WARNING level on first failure, then back off to DEBUG for repeated failures. At minimum: logger.warning('VPN check error: %s', e)

findings.title: UNKNOWN VPN state breaks duplicate-notification suppression
findings.description: When check_vpn_status() returns VpnStatus.UNKNOWN, last_vpn is updated to UNKNOWN. On the next tick, if VPN is still NOT_DETECTED, the condition 'last_vpn != VpnStatus.NOT_DETECTED' evaluates True (UNKNOWN != NOT_DETECTED), causing a duplicate disconnect notification. Per spec: UNKNOWN should be treated as 'no change' — last_vpn should NOT be updated when vpn == UNKNOWN.
findings.impact: User receives repeated 'VPN disconnected' warnings while VPN is already known to be down, if an UNKNOWN state appears between two NOT_DETECTED states.

findings.title: Lazy import of STATE_FILE inside conditional hot path
findings.description: The import 'from src.system_config import STATE_FILE' is placed inside the watchdog-dead conditional in _monitor_tick(). While Python caches module imports in sys.modules, this duplicates an import that already appears at line 582 (inside main()). If the module import path differs between the two call sites or the module is reloaded, STATE_FILE may resolve to different values. Additionally, readability is harmed by imports buried inside logic.
findings.impact: Low risk, but code clarity and import consistency are reduced.
findings.recommendation: Move 'from src.system_config import STATE_FILE' to module-level imports at the top of main.py alongside other imports.
